edges.description: IDS deficiency directly causes progressive intracellular storage of its glycosaminoglycan substrates.
edges.target: Heparan sulfate and dermatan sulfate lysosomal accumulation

edges.description: The IDS enzymatic lesion is measured diagnostically as deficient iduronate-2-sulfatase activity in patient cells or plasma.
Persistent substrate storage disrupts lysosomal homeostasis and produces broader cellular dysfunction.
edges.source: Heparan sulfate and dermatan sulfate lysosomal accumulation

edges.description: Visceral glycosaminoglycan storage contributes to enlarged liver and spleen volume.
intermediate_mechanisms: visceral lysosomal storage and organ enlargement
edges.source: Heparan sulfate and dermatan sulfate lysosomal accumulation

edges.description: Quantitative urinary glycosaminoglycan excretion is a diagnostic and pharmacodynamic readout of the systemic storage burden.
edges.source: Heparan sulfate and dermatan sulfate lysosomal accumulation

edges.description: Heparan sulfate is elevated in CSF in neuronopathic MPS II and reflects central nervous system substrate accumulation.
edges.source: Heparan sulfate and dermatan sulfate lysosomal accumulation

edges.description: Dermatan sulfate is also elevated in CSF in neuronopathic MPS II and reports the second primary IDS substrate.
edges.source: Heparan sulfate and dermatan sulfate lysosomal accumulation

edges.description: Persistent lysosomal dysfunction is associated with measurable neuronal damage and degeneration in neuronopathic disease.
GM3 ganglioside elevation in CSF reports secondary lysosomal lipid storage and dysfunction in the neuronopathic CNS branch.
Storage-driven cellular dysfunction propagates into joints, cartilage, growth plates, and connective tissue.
Soft-tissue storage and thickening drive progressive upper airway disease.
intermediate_mechanisms: glycosaminoglycan storage and thickening in upper-airway soft tissues

edges.description: Persistent glycosaminoglycan burden disrupts valve extracellular matrix homeostasis and drives valvular disease.
Secondary neuronal injury contributes to progressive cognitive and motor decline in severe disease.
Elevated neurofilament light chain in CSF and serum reports axonal injury and active neurodegeneration in neuronopathic MPS II.
The severe neuronopathic form manifests clinically as progressive cognitive impairment.
Communicating hydrocephalus is a recognized CNS manifestation that can accompany macrocephaly in MPS II.
intermediate_mechanisms: communicating hydrocephalus in the neuronopathic CNS branch

edges.description: Joint and connective-tissue GAG storage manifests clinically as progressive joint stiffness.
Skeletal and cartilage involvement contributes to growth impairment and short stature.
Cranial skeletal involvement and communicating hydrocephalus can manifest as macrocephaly or large head circumference.
intermediate_mechanisms: cranial skeletal and communicating-hydrocephalus contributions to head size

edges.description: Skeletal and connective-tissue disease can narrow the spinal canal and cause spinal stenosis.
intermediate_mechanisms: skeletal and connective-tissue narrowing of the spinal canal

edges.description: Connective-tissue thickening around the wrist can produce median nerve compression clinically recognized as carpal tunnel syndrome.
intermediate_mechanisms: median nerve entrapment in glycosaminoglycan-thickened wrist connective tissue

edges.description: Progressive airway disease evolves from rhinitis and noisy breathing to sleep apnea.
Leaflet thickening and stiffening produce the structural cardiac valve phenotype.

edges.target: Heparan sulfate and dermatan sulfate lysosomal accumulation

edges.description: Decreased iduronate-2-sulfatase activity reports the root lysosomal enzyme defect in Hunter syndrome.
Abnormal urinary GAG excretion reports systemic glycosaminoglycan storage.
edges.source: Heparan sulfate and dermatan sulfate lysosomal accumulation

edges.description: Falling urinary GAGs report treatment-lowered systemic storage burden.
edges.source: Heparan sulfate and dermatan sulfate lysosomal accumulation

edges.description: Elevated CSF heparan sulfate reports CNS substrate storage in neuronopathic disease.
edges.source: Heparan sulfate and dermatan sulfate lysosomal accumulation

edges.description: Elevated CSF dermatan sulfate reports CNS substrate storage in neuronopathic disease.
edges.source: Heparan sulfate and dermatan sulfate lysosomal accumulation

edges.description: Elevated CSF GM3 reports secondary lysosomal lipid storage beyond primary GAG accumulation.
Elevated neurofilament light chain reports active axonal injury and neurodegeneration in the CNS disease branch.

nodes.description: Progressive rhinitis, noisy breathing, and sleep apnea reflect glycosaminoglycan storage in upper-airway soft tissues and related ENT structures.

evidence.snippet: Recurrent and prolonged rhinitis with persistent nasal discharge are the first symptoms of airway disease that manifests itself as noisy breathing and later sleep apnea.

evidence.reference_title: Characterization of Fluid Biomarkers Reveals Lysosome Dysfunction and Neurodegeneration in Neuronopathic MPS II Patients.
evidence.snippet: Targeted analysis of GM3, BMP, and glucosylceramides shows that nMPS II patient CSF (regardless of therapy) have elevated levels of these lipids (Figure 7A,D,G), and that there is a strong correlation between HS and lipids levels in the CSF (Figure 7B,E,H).

readouts.interpretation: Elevated CSF GM3 reports secondary lysosomal lipid storage beyond primary GAG accumulation.

evidence.reference_title: Characterization of Fluid Biomarkers Reveals Lysosome Dysfunction and Neurodegeneration in Neuronopathic MPS II Patients.
evidence.snippet: we observed a significant, 11-fold and 30-fold elevation in HS and DS levels, respectively in nMPS II patient CSF relative to the control group (Figure 4A,B).

readouts.interpretation: Elevated CSF dermatan sulfate reports CNS substrate storage in neuronopathic disease.
readouts.target: Heparan sulfate and dermatan sulfate lysosomal accumulation

evidence.reference_title: Characterization of Fluid Biomarkers Reveals Lysosome Dysfunction and Neurodegeneration in Neuronopathic MPS II Patients.
evidence.snippet: we observed a significant, 11-fold and 30-fold elevation in HS and DS levels, respectively in nMPS II patient CSF relative to the control group (Figure 4A,B).

readouts.interpretation: Elevated CSF heparan sulfate reports CNS substrate storage in neuronopathic disease.
readouts.target: Heparan sulfate and dermatan sulfate lysosomal accumulation

nodes.description: Progressive valvular thickening causes clinically important regurgitation and stenosis in Hunter syndrome.

evidence.snippet: valvular involvement, with progressive thickening and stiffening of the valve leaflets leading to mitral and aortic regurgitation and stenosis .

nodes.description: Dermatan-sulfate-rich valve extracellular matrix accumulates glycosaminoglycans, producing progressive leaflet thickening, regurgitation, and stenosis.

evidence.reference_title: Natural history of valve disease in patients with mucopolysaccharidosis II and the impact of enzyme replacement therapy.
evidence.snippet: DS is a major component of the extracellular matrix of heart valves, which can be affected in MPS II.
valvular involvement, with progressive thickening and stiffening of the valve leaflets leading to mitral and aortic regurgitation and stenosis .

nodes.description: Carpal tunnel syndrome reflects constrictive median neuropathy at the wrist and is a recognized peripheral nerve entrapment complication in MPS II.

evidence.snippet: Additional findings in neuronopathic and non-neuronopathic MPS II include: short stature, macrocephaly with or without communicating hydrocephalus, macroglossia, hoarse voice, conductive and sensorineural hearing loss, dysostosis multiplex, spinal stenosis, carpal tunnel syndrome, and hepatosplenomegaly.

nodes.description: Severe neuronopathic disease causes progressive cognitive impairment, whereas attenuated forms may preserve cognition.

evidence.reference_title: Mortality and cause of death in mucopolysaccharidosis type II-a historical review based on data from the Hunter Outcome Survey (HOS).
evidence.snippet: Patients with the severe form of the disease have cognitive impairment and typically die in the second decade of life.

nodes.description: Accumulated glycosaminoglycans in joints, cartilage, and connective tissue drive progressive stiffness, growth impairment, and skeletal pathology.

evidence.reference_title: Mucopolysaccharidosis type II: skeletal-muscle system involvement.
evidence.snippet: Lysosomal accumulation of these GAG molecules results in cell, tissue, and organ dysfunction. The skeletal-muscle system involvement is because of essential accumulated GAGs in joints and connective tissue.

evidence.reference_title: Lentiviral Gene Therapy for Mucopolysaccharidosis II with Tagged Iduronate 2-Sulfatase Prevents Life-Threatening Pathology in Peripheral Tissues But Fails to Correct Cartilage.
evidence.snippet: In contrast, tracheal, epiphyseal, and articular cartilage remained largely uncorrected by all vectors tested.

nodes.description: Hearing loss is common in severe disease and contributes to the ENT burden of Hunter syndrome.

evidence.snippet: Children with severe form, diagnosed at 12-36 months, have coarse facial feature, short stature, joint stiffness, short neck, broad chest, large head circumference, watery diarrhea, skeletal changes, progressive and profound mental retardation, retinal degeneration' hearing loss, cardiomyopathy, valvular involvement, with progressive thickening and stiffening of the valve leaflets leading to mitral and aortic regurgitation and stenosis .

nodes.description: IDS insufficiency leads to excess heparan sulfate and dermatan sulfate within lysosomes across multiple tissues and organs, creating the primary storage burden that initiates downstream disease biology.
nodes.id: Heparan sulfate and dermatan sulfate lysosomal accumulation

evidence.reference_title: Natural progression of neurological disease in mucopolysaccharidosis type II.
evidence.snippet: Mucopolysaccharidosis type II (MPS II) is a lysosomal storage disorder characterized by insufficiency of the iduronate-2-sulfatase enzyme, which results in excess heparan and dermatan sulfates within the lysosomes of various tissues and organs, including the central nervous system.

nodes.description: Increased liver and spleen volume is part of the multisystem visceral storage burden of Hunter syndrome.

evidence.snippet: Inguinal and umbilical hernias occur caused by the disturbed structure of connective tissue and increased liver and spleen volume.

nodes.description: Communicating hydrocephalus can accompany macrocephaly in MPS II and is part of the recognized cranial/CNS phenotype.

evidence.snippet: Additional findings in neuronopathic and non-neuronopathic MPS II include: short stature, macrocephaly with or without communicating hydrocephalus, macroglossia, hoarse voice, conductive and sensorineural hearing loss, dysostosis multiplex, spinal stenosis, carpal tunnel syndrome, and hepatosplenomegaly.
IDS | HGNC:5389 | mucopolysaccharidosis type 2 | MONDO:0010674 | XL | Definitive

Hunter syndrome is caused by deficiency of the lysososmal enzyme iduronate-2-sulphatase that cleaves O-linked sulphate moieties from dermatan sulphate and heparan sulphate and leads to accumulation of GAGs.

readouts.interpretation: Decreased iduronate-2-sulfatase activity reports the root lysosomal enzyme defect in Hunter syndrome.

nodes.description: Pathogenic IDS variants reduce or abolish iduronate-2-sulfatase activity, blocking normal lysosomal degradation of dermatan sulfate and heparan sulfate.

evidence.snippet: Hunter syndrome is caused by deficiency of the lysososmal enzyme iduronate-2-sulphatase that cleaves O-linked sulphate moieties from dermatan sulphate and heparan sulphate and leads to accumulation of GAGs.

nodes.description: Intravenous recombinant iduronate-2-sulfatase is the standard disease-modifying therapy for peripheral somatic disease. It lowers urinary glycosaminoglycans, reduces organomegaly, and can improve endurance, but treatment effects are incomplete and CNS disease is not corrected.

evidence.reference_title: A phase I/II clinical trial of enzyme replacement therapy in mucopolysaccharidosis II (Hunter syndrome).
evidence.snippet: Urinary glycosaminoglycans were reduced within 2 weeks of initiating idursulfase and were decreased 49% after 48 weeks of treatment (P<0.0001). Both liver and spleen volume were decreased at 24 weeks (P<0.01) and 48 weeks (P<0.001). The 6-minute walk test distance increased an average of 48 meters after 48 weeks (P=0.013).

evidence.reference_title: Clinical efficacy of enzyme replacement therapy in paediatric Hunter patients, an independent study of 3.5 years.
evidence.snippet: Although the present protocol of idursulfase administration may result efficacious in delaying the MPS II somatic disease progression at some extent, in this study we observed that several signs and symptoms did not improve during the therapy.
Current research is focused on pharmacological chaperones, gene therapy and substrate reduction therapy and therapies that, unlike Idursulfase, do cross the blood-brain barrier.

nodes.description: Progressive joint stiffness reflects glycosaminoglycan storage in joints and connective tissue.

evidence.snippet: Children with severe form, diagnosed at 12-36 months, have coarse facial feature, short stature, joint stiffness, short neck, broad chest, large head circumference, watery diarrhea, skeletal changes, progressive and profound mental retardation, retinal degeneration' hearing loss, cardiomyopathy, valvular involvement, with progressive thickening and stiffening of the valve leaflets leading to mitral and aortic regurgitation and stenosis .

nodes.description: Storage burden progresses beyond primary glycosaminoglycan accumulation to broader lysosomal dysfunction, including glial lysosomal aggregation in IDS-deficient neural cells and evidence of abnormal lysosomal lipid burden in neuronopathic patients.

evidence.reference_title: Murine neural stem cells model Hunter disease in vitro: glial cell-mediated neurodegeneration as a possible mechanism involved.
evidence.snippet: Experiments here reported show that NSCs derived from the subventricular zone (SVZ) of early symptomatic IDS-knockout (IDS-ko) mouse retained self-renewal capacity in vitro, but differentiated earlier than wild-type (wt) cells, displaying an evident lysosomal aggregation in oligodendroglial and astroglial cells.

evidence.reference_title: Characterization of Fluid Biomarkers Reveals Lysosome Dysfunction and Neurodegeneration in Neuronopathic MPS II Patients.
evidence.snippet: In addition to the accumulation of CSF GAGs, nMPS II patients show elevated levels of lysosomal lipids, neurofilament light chain, and other biomarkers of neuronal damage and degeneration.

nodes.description: Macrocephaly or large head circumference can occur in MPS II, sometimes in association with communicating hydrocephalus.

evidence.snippet: Additional findings in neuronopathic and non-neuronopathic MPS II include: short stature, macrocephaly with or without communicating hydrocephalus, macroglossia, hoarse voice, conductive and sensorineural hearing loss, dysostosis multiplex, spinal stenosis, carpal tunnel syndrome, and hepatosplenomegaly.

nodes.description: Hunter syndrome remains a high-burden multisystem disease even in treated adults, so ongoing respiratory, ENT/audiologic, orthopedic, cardiology, and surgical management is required alongside disease-directed therapy.

evidence.reference_title: Unmet needs of adults living with mucopolysaccharidosis II: data from the Hunter Outcome Survey.
evidence.snippet: Overall, adult patients with neuronopathic and non-neuronopathic MPS II had a high disease burden and requirement for surgeries, emphasizing the need to continue multidisciplinary management and regular assessments in adulthood.

evidence.reference_title: Characterization of Fluid Biomarkers Reveals Lysosome Dysfunction and Neurodegeneration in Neuronopathic MPS II Patients.
evidence.snippet: We measured levels of Nf-L in the CSF and serum and found a significant elevation in Nf-L in both the CSF and serum of nMPS II patients regardless of their treatment regimen (Figure 8A,C), and that Nf-L levels positively correlated with CSF HS (Figure 8B).

readouts.interpretation: Elevated neurofilament light chain reports active axonal injury and neurodegeneration in the CNS disease branch.

nodes.description: Severe Hunter syndrome causes progressive central nervous system disease with decline in cognitive, language, adaptive, and motor functions rather than stable neurodevelopment.

evidence.reference_title: Natural progression of neurological disease in mucopolysaccharidosis type II.
evidence.snippet: One subset of patients had normal cognitive, speech and language, and adaptive functions whereas the other showed a dramatic decline in these areas.

evidence.reference_title: Mortality and cause of death in mucopolysaccharidosis type II-a historical review based on data from the Hunter Outcome Survey (HOS).
evidence.snippet: Patients with the severe form of the disease have cognitive impairment and typically die in the second decade of life.

nodes.description: In neuronopathic MPS II, lysosomal dysfunction is accompanied by measurable biomarkers of neuronal damage and degeneration, establishing a distinct downstream neuroinjury step before overt clinical CNS decline.

evidence.reference_title: Characterization of Fluid Biomarkers Reveals Lysosome Dysfunction and Neurodegeneration in Neuronopathic MPS II Patients.
evidence.snippet: In addition to the accumulation of CSF GAGs, nMPS II patients show elevated levels of lysosomal lipids, neurofilament light chain, and other biomarkers of neuronal damage and degeneration.

nodes.description: Growth failure develops as connective-tissue and skeletal disease progresses.

evidence.snippet: Children with severe form, diagnosed at 12-36 months, have coarse facial feature, short stature, joint stiffness, short neck, broad chest, large head circumference, watery diarrhea, skeletal changes, progressive and profound mental retardation, retinal degeneration' hearing loss, cardiomyopathy, valvular involvement, with progressive thickening and stiffening of the valve leaflets leading to mitral and aortic regurgitation and stenosis .

nodes.description: Progressive upper-airway disease commonly evolves into noisy breathing and sleep apnea.

evidence.snippet: Recurrent and prolonged rhinitis with persistent nasal discharge are the first symptoms of airway disease that manifests itself as noisy breathing and later sleep apnea.

nodes.description: Spinal canal stenosis is part of the skeletal/connective-tissue burden in MPS II and can require neurosurgical or orthopedic management.

evidence.snippet: Additional findings in neuronopathic and non-neuronopathic MPS II include: short stature, macrocephaly with or without communicating hydrocephalus, macroglossia, hoarse voice, conductive and sensorineural hearing loss, dysostosis multiplex, spinal stenosis, carpal tunnel syndrome, and hepatosplenomegaly.

nodes.description: Tividenofusp alfa-eknm is a CNS-penetrant enzyme replacement therapy approved for MPS II, extending enzyme replacement to the neuronopathic disease branch that standard intravenous idursulfase does not adequately address.

evidence.snippet: Weekly enzyme replacement therapy (ERT) with infusions of idursulfase, a recombinant form of human I2S, or tividenofusp alfa-eknm, a CNS-penetrant ERT, are approved for individuals with MPS II.
Sceening is by quantitative assessment of urinary GAGs excretion.

readouts.interpretation: Abnormal urinary GAG excretion reports systemic glycosaminoglycan storage.
readouts.target: Heparan sulfate and dermatan sulfate lysosomal accumulation

readouts.interpretation: Falling urinary GAGs report treatment-lowered systemic storage burden.
readouts.target: Heparan sulfate and dermatan sulfate lysosomal accumulation
